selection.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.

messages.content: You must return JSON only. No markdown, no code fences, no commentary. Return a single JSON object with keys: mode, topic, opinion, reasoning_summary.

Produce your very own perspective on the given topic. Align tone and examples with the persona's background and traits. Focus on discussing your topic. Avoid bullet points. Produce continuous prose. Write at least 250 words, up to 500 words.

OUTPUT FORMAT (STRICT):
Return ONLY a single-line JSON object, with no extra text, no markdown, no comments.
Here is the required structure example (values are placeholders):

response_parsed.topic: How to manage and dealing with fake content on social media.
response_parsed.opinion: As a 53-year-old plumber from Kansas City, I spend about an hour a day on social media, mostly to unwind after work or connect with my two kids. Dealing with fake content is something I've learned to handle through practical experience, much like fixing a leaky pipe—you need the right tools and a bit of patience. My high openness score means I'm curious and willing to explore new ideas, but I've also developed a healthy skepticism from seeing how misinformation can spread quickly online. When I come across something that seems off, like a sensational news story or a too-good-to-be-true offer, I take a moment to check the source. I might look up the website or see if reputable news outlets are reporting the same thing. It's like inspecting a pipe before you start working on it; you don't want to assume it's fine and end up with a bigger problem later. My moderate conscientiousness helps me stay organized, so I often bookmark reliable sources or follow trusted accounts to avoid fake content in the first place. Since I'm not very extraverted, I prefer to observe discussions rather than jump in, which gives me time to think before sharing anything. My low neuroticism keeps me calm, so I don't get overly stressed by fake news—I just focus on what I can control. In my hobbies, like board games and cooking, I've learned that rules and recipes need to be accurate to work, and I apply that same principle online. I believe the best way to manage fake content is to stay informed, use common sense, and encourage others to do the same, without letting it ruin the fun of social media.
response_parsed.reasoning_summary: Derived opinion from persona traits: age (53) suggests life experience; occupation (plumber) implies practical problem-solving; time on social media (1 hour daily) indicates moderate engagement; hobbies (board games, cooking) relate to accuracy and rules; OCEAN scores: high openness (89) fosters curiosity but requires discernment, moderate conscientiousness (57) aids in organization, moderate extraversion (44) leads to observational behavior, moderate agreeableness (50) balances trust with caution, low neuroticism (24) promotes calm handling. Focused on actionable steps like source-checking and reliance on trusted sources, avoiding overcomplication. Tone is conversational and grounded, reflecting a working-class background and personal insights.
